specific_change: Switch the payment-provider integration from basic VPC peering to AWS PrivateLink and implement a retry policy with exponential backoff on outbound payment-provider calls. Do not scale compute — the instances are correctly sized and the bottleneck is at the network boundary.

conclusion.headline: Replace VPC peering with PrivateLink + retries to fix network-driven SLA breach

telemetry_observations: Network P95 latency spikes to 233.7 ms at p95, with max 239.6 ms, vs a baseline p50 of 52.9 ms.
Application P95 latency reaches 1015.1 ms at p95 (max 1069.8 ms), breaching the 300 ms SLA target by >3×.
Network error rate peaks at 0.015 (p95 = 0.013), well above the ~0.002 baseline.
Compute CPU p95 is 67.7% at the 95th percentile — well within capacity headroom.
Memory p95 peaks at 62%, indicating compute is not resource-constrained.
Application latency stddev of 344 ms signals extreme variance correlated with business-hours spikes.
infrastructure_context: Payment-provider integration uses basic VPC peering with no retry policy configured.
Compute ASG runs 6× m5.large instances (min 6, max 10) with target-tracking scaling — correctly sized for current CPU/memory load.
Network tier uses a round-robin NLB with no PrivateLink endpoint defined in Terraform.
correlation_observations: Pearson coefficient of 0.976 (lag 0 min) between network_p95_latency_ms and application_p95_latency_ms confirms network latency directly drives application latency with no delay.

reasoning: The near-perfect correlation (r=0.976, zero lag) between network and application P95 latency proves the network tier is the root cause of the SLA breach. Compute resources are healthy — CPU p95 at ~68% and memory at ~62% — so scaling instances would not address the problem. The basic VPC peering path to the payment provider introduces high-variance latency (stddev 75 ms) and elevated error rates during business hours. Replacing this with AWS PrivateLink provides a dedicated, low-latency path that avoids cross-region peering congestion, while adding retries with exponential backoff handles transient failures gracefully without cascading into application-level timeouts.

projected_state.notes: Based on before/after evidence: network P95 expected to drop below 80 ms during business hours, bringing application P95 well under the 300 ms SLA target. Compute utilization unchanged.

cost_impact.notes: PrivateLink endpoint costs ~$7.50/mo per AZ (2 AZs = $15) plus $0.01/GB data processing. Estimated net increase of ~$100-$200/mo depending on throughput. This is a reliability investment, not a cost-saving measure — it eliminates SLA breaches that could incur far greater penalty costs.

risk_assessment.primary_risk: PrivateLink endpoint misconfiguration or payment-provider service not supporting PrivateLink could cause a connectivity outage during migration.
risk_assessment.mitigation: Deploy PrivateLink endpoint alongside existing VPC peering and shift traffic gradually using weighted DNS or feature flags. Monitor network_p95_latency_ms and error_rate on the new path before decommissioning peering. Set alarms on error rate > 0.005 and latency > 100 ms.
risk_assessment.rollback: Revert DNS or feature flag to route traffic back through the existing VPC peering path. The peering connection remains active until the new path is validated and stable for at least one full business-week cycle.
risk_assessment.notes: Retry policy should cap at 3 attempts with jitter to avoid thundering-herd effects on the payment provider. Circuit breaker pattern recommended as a complement.
